skill.name: wordpress-dev
skill.description: WordPress development guidance for themes and plugins, including coding standards, security checks, custom post types, hooks, REST APIs, performance, and templates.
skill.summary: Build WordPress themes and plugins with secure, standards-based guidance.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: AI review found most of the 555 static alerts are documentation or template false positives, especially markdown backticks, WordPress helper names, and example URLs. Two generated PHP templates contain moderate copy-paste risks: REST meta values are saved without explicit sanitization, and taxonomy custom field saves lack nonce verification. No prompt injection or malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked but should be published with a security warning.

medium_findings.title: REST Template Saves Custom Meta Without Explicit Sanitization
medium_findings.description: The generated REST endpoint template writes price, location, and featured request parameters directly to post meta in create and update paths. Permission callbacks exist, but these meta parameters are not declared with sanitize callbacks before persistence.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write paths are direct and semantic context confirms they persist request-controlled values. Legitimate use is possible after adding field-specific validation and sanitization.

medium_findings.title: Taxonomy Custom Field Save Lacks Nonce Verification
medium_findings.description: The taxonomy template saves custom term fields from POST data with sanitization, but the save handler does not verify a nonce before updating term meta. WordPress capability checks help, but nonce verification is still expected for admin form intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The handler clearly reads POST data and updates term meta without a visible nonce check in the template. Impact is moderate because values are sanitized and WordPress admin permissions still apply.

low_findings.title: Scaffold Script Writes Files to User-Selected Paths
low_findings.description: The scaffold script creates the requested output directory and writes generated PHP files there. This is expected functionality, but users should avoid running it with untrusted arguments or privileged output paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem behavior is confirmed in the script and is legitimate for a code generator. Risk depends on how the user invokes the script.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick and command substitution alerts are caused by markdown fences, inline WordPress examples, and shell commands shown as documentation. I did not find runtime shell execution in the scaffold script or templates.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed examples are fenced documentation, and the executable Python script does not invoke subprocess or shell APIs. This strongly supports a false-positive verdict for the command-execution cluster.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Alerts Are Mostly WordPress Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts align with WordPress terms, placeholders, markdown text, and helper names rather than confirmed MD5, SHA1, or custom crypto usage. No evidence found of cryptographic routines used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited areas contain metadata, constants, or security guidance, not weak hashing logic. Confidence is high, though this summarizes a large static cluster rather than every individual line.

low_findings.title: Network Alerts Are External References and WordPress API Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs, fetch examples, email references, and REST examples are documentation or local WordPress integration patterns. No evidence found of credential exfiltration or unauthorized outbound network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed network indicators are examples or official documentation links. The skill does not contain code that sends secrets to an external endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Request-Controlled Values Persisted to Post Meta
dangerous_patterns.description: The REST template persists request parameters to post meta without field-specific sanitization at the point of write.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a direct persistence pattern with request-derived values. The risk is moderate because route permissions are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: POST-Based Term Meta Update Without Nonce Check
dangerous_patterns.description: The taxonomy template updates term meta from POST data without showing nonce verification in the save handler.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The missing nonce is visible in the handler context. Sanitization lowers impact but does not verify form intent.

content.user_title: Build Safer WordPress Themes and Plugins
content.value_statement: WordPress development requires secure patterns across hooks, REST endpoints, custom post types, and performance work. This skill gives Codex, Claude, and Claude Code focused WordPress guidance and boilerplate.
actual_capabilities: Explains WordPress PHP, JavaScript, and CSS coding standards with examples.
Provides templates for custom post types, taxonomies, meta boxes, REST endpoints, and plugin skeletons.
Guides sanitization, escaping, nonce verification, capability checks, and prepared SQL usage.
Covers hooks, filters, template hierarchy, caching, asset loading, and query performance.
Includes a scaffold script that generates boilerplate from local template files.
Recommends common WordPress plugin categories and development tooling.
limitations: Generated templates require review before use in production WordPress sites.
The scaffold script does not validate every placeholder for WordPress naming limits.
Guidance is broad and may need adjustment for a site-specific architecture.
It does not run automated WordPress, PHPCS, PHPUnit, or browser tests by itself.

use_cases.title: Create WordPress Content Models
use_cases.description: Generate and adapt custom post type, taxonomy, and meta box patterns for a theme or child theme.

use_cases.title: Review Plugin Security
use_cases.description: Check form handlers, REST routes, SQL queries, and output rendering against WordPress security practices.

use_cases.title: Improve Site Performance
use_cases.description: Audit queries, cache strategy, asset loading, image handling, and cron usage for performance issues.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Custom Post Type
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a WordPress custom post type for [content type]. Include labels, REST support, menu icon, and rewrite settings.

prompt_templates.title: Add Secure Meta Fields
prompt_templates.prompt: Add secure meta fields for [post type]. Include nonce checks, capability checks, sanitization, and escaped admin output.

prompt_templates.title: Review a REST Endpoint
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this WordPress REST endpoint for permission, validation, sanitization, and information exposure risks: [paste endpoint].

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a WordPress Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this WordPress feature for query, caching, asset loading, and cron performance issues. Suggest prioritized fixes: [paste code].

output_examples.input: Create a portfolio custom post type with project categories.
output: A registration plan for the portfolio post type.
Recommended labels, supports, REST settings, and rewrite behavior.
Security notes for generated admin fields and archive output.

output_examples.input: Review this plugin settings form for security.
output: A checklist of missing nonce, capability, sanitization, and escaping controls.
Plain-language severity notes for each issue.
A safer implementation plan aligned with WordPress APIs.

output_examples.input: Improve performance for a slow property listing page.
output: Asset loading and image optimization recommendations.

best_practices: Ask for a security review before copying generated PHP templates into production.
Run PHPCS with WordPress coding standards after adapting generated code.
Replace placeholders with project-specific prefixes, text domains, capabilities, and validation rules.
anti_patterns: Do not treat scaffolded templates as production-ready without review.
Do not expose private post data through public REST routes.
Do not bypass WordPress APIs with raw SQL, direct file edits, or core modifications.

faq.question: Does this skill write code automatically?
faq.answer: It includes a scaffold script that can write local boilerplate files when the user runs it.

faq.question: Can it build complete WordPress plugins?
faq.answer: It provides starter templates and guidance, but production plugins still need integration and testing.

faq.question: Does it cover WordPress security?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers sanitization, escaping, nonces, capabilities, SQL safety, uploads, AJAX, and REST API checks.

faq.question: Is the generated REST API template production-ready?
faq.answer: No. The REST template needs project-specific validation, sanitization, and data exposure review.

faq.question: Does it support block themes?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes notes on theme.json, block templates, template parts, and modern WordPress theme structure.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
